coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of self-quenching-resistant biomass-based solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with high yield for white lighting emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Min
Huang, Xun
Ni, Jiaxin
Han, Youqi
Zhang, Chunlei
Li, Shujun
Cao, Jun
Li, Jian
Chen, Zhijun
Han, Shiyan
coredata.dc:description: A strategy, based on introducing steric hindrance, has been designed to prepare biomass-based solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (SSF-CDs) with high yield (~100%) via one-pot hydrothermal reaction of dehydroabietic acid and ethanolamine. The obtained SSF-CDs emitted bright light-yellow fluorescence under UV light, were resistant to aggregation-caused quenching and had a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 10%. Further, the structural composition and optical properties of the SSF-CDs were investigated in detail, which contained a graphitic carbon core, a sterically hindered non-planar tricyclic phenanthrene skeleton and a variety of functional groups, simultaneously. The sterically hindered phenanthrene skeleton efficiently suppressed π-π interactions between carbon cores in the SSF-CDs, thus enabled solid-state emission. Importantly, the biomass-based SSF-CDs can be used as the color conversion layer in a white light-emitting diode (WLED), with CIE coordinates of (0.3304, 0.3055), CCT of 5608 K and CRI of 88.6.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dehydroabietic acid
Solid-state fluorescence emission
High yield
White lighting emitting diode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of WLED based on SSF-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of SSF-CDs (inset: high resolution TEM images of SSF-CDs); (b) Size distribution of SSF-CDs; (c) FT-IR spectra of dehydroabietic acid and SSF-CDs; (d) 1H NMR spectrum of dehydroabietic acid; (e) 1H NMR spectrum of SSF-CDs; (f) Full XPS spectrum of SSF-CDs; (g–i) O1s, C1s and N1s XPS spectra of SSF-CDs.

(a) PL emission spectra of powdered SSF-CDs (inset: photograph of SSF-CDs under UV light); (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of SSF-CDs (Excitation wavelength = 440 nm); (c) Fluorescence decay curves of powdered SSF-CDs.

(a) PL emission spectra of solution of SSF-CDs in ethanol (1 mg mL−1); (b) Fluorescence decay curves of solution of SSF-CDs in ethanol (1 mg mL−1); (c) Fluorescence spectra of ethanol solutions of SSF-CDs with different concentrations; (d) CIE chromaticity diagram of ethanol solutions of SSF-CDs, with concentrations from 1 mg mL−1 to 30 mg mL−1; (e) Fluorescence intensity of ethanol solutions of SSF-CDs with different concentrations; (f) Schematic illustration of mechanism for solid-state emission of SSF-CDs; (g) Schematic illustration of energy transfer of SSF-CDs.

(a) Schematic showing WLED based on SSF-CDs, operating at 20 mA; (b) CIE color coordinates of WLED fabricated using SSF-CDs as color conversion layer, with different weight/weight ratios of SSF-CDs to epoxy resin (inset: photograph of WLED operating at 20 mA); (c) Electroluminescence spectrum of WLED based on weight/weight ratio of SSF-CDs to epoxy resin of 1:3; (d–e) Photographs of WLED off and on. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Preparation of solid-state fluorescent emission carbon dots (SSF-CDs) from dehydroabietic acid and ethanolamine.
Comparison of yield of solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with previously reported yields.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of self-quenching-resistant biomass-based solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with high yield for white lighting emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Harbin, 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Postdoctoral Station of Mechanical Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Postdoctoral Station of Mechanical Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-Based Material Science & Technology (Northeast Forestry University)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A strategy, based on introducing steric hindrance, has been designed to prepare biomass-based solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (SSF-CDs) with high yield (~100%) via one-pot hydrothermal reaction of dehydroabietic acid and ethanolamine. The obtained SSF-CDs emitted bright light-yellow fluorescence under UV light, were resistant to aggregation-caused quenching and had a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 10%. Further, the structural composition and optical properties of the SSF-CDs were investigated in detail, which contained a graphitic carbon core, a sterically hindered non-planar tricyclic phenanthrene skeleton and a variety of functional groups, simultaneously. The sterically hindered phenanthrene skeleton efficiently suppressed π-π interactions between carbon cores in the SSF-CDs, thus enabled solid-state emission. Importantly, the biomass-based SSF-CDs can be used as the color conversion layer in a white light-emitting diode (WLED), with CIE coordinates of (0.3304, 0.3055), CCT of 5608 K and CRI of 88.6.

High-yielding (~100%) synthesis of biomass-based solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with distinct advantages for solid-state luminescent devices.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) are currently attracting much attention because of their exceptional properties, which include fast response, low power consumption, high luminous efficiency and long lifetime [1–4]. Therefore, distinguishing traditional lighting technology, the superior properties promote WLED to be a promising candidate for a new generation of solid-state light sources [5]. While WLEDs are designed, the choice of solid-state phosphor, which is adopted in the color conversion layer, is critical for determining whether a WLED conform to the requirements for low energy consumption and positive environmental effects. A variety of solid-state fluorescent materials, including rare earth-based phosphors [6,7], graphene quantum dots [8], semiconductor and perovskite quantum dots [9–12] and solid-state fluorescent carbon dots (SSF-CDs) [13–16], have been employed to construct WLEDs. SSF-CDs, in particular, are now attracting much interest because rare earth-based phosphors, semiconductor quantum dots and perovskite quantum dots, which are popular materials for the color conversion layers of WLEDs, have significant drawbacks, including long term toxicity, environmental risks and high cost [17–19]. SSF-CDs stand out among the many solid-state fluorescent materials because of their stable fluorescence, ease of preparation, easily tunable emission, low toxicity and low cost [20–23].

The precursors for the preparation of SSF-CDs is diverse, such as polymeric macromolecules [24], small organic molecules [25–27] and biomass materials [28,29]. However, many organic molecules are, expensive and present risks to the natural environment and human health [30–32]. Biomass precursors and their derivatives are receiving particular attention because they are renewable, cheap and environmentally friendly [31,33,34]. Although biomass-based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) can be prepared from cheap raw materials, and their fluorescence is very stable, some problems still remain [35]. Most CDs are prepared by hydrothermal treatment of biomass materials. Such reactions typically require a purification step, which is time-consuming, yields reduction and costs increment [36–38]. Other potential obstacles to employ CDs directly as the color conversion layer for WLEDs are excessive resonance energy transfer (RET) and direct π-π interactions between carbon cores, which lead to solid-state CDs suffering from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [39,40]. Most reports describe liquid-state biomass-based CDs [28,41–44], whereas very few cares about biomass-based solid-state CDs being adopted to prepare WLEDs. The biomass-derived CDs not only required purification steps, but also needed to be embedded in a matrix to obtain solid-state fluorescent materials that could be used to prepare WLEDs [45–48], both of which are time-consuming and costly. A fast, simple method for the large-scale preparation of self-quenching-resistant SSF-CDs from easily available and low-cost biomass-based materials would thus be very valuable and would help to protect the environment.

In this work, a high-yielding (~100%), one-pot hydrothermal process, using dehydroabietic acid and ethanolamine, was developed for the preparation of bright light-yellow SSF-CDs, which had a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 10%. Contrast to biomass-based CDs with ACQ, or solid-state CDs, the preparation of SSF-CDs is “green”, and free from cumbersome separation and purification steps (Scheme 1 ). The high yield makes this strategy very suitable for large-scale production of SSF-CDs at low cost. Importantly, the use of the SSF-CDs as the color conversion layer produced a WLED with CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage, International Commission on Illumination) chromaticity coordinates (0.3304, 0.3055).

Dehydroabietic acid (99.06%) used in this work was purchased from Jusheng Technology Co., Ltd. (Hubei, China). The ethanolamine and anhydrous ethanol were analytical grade. All reagents were used as received without further purification, all solvents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was produced using a Clever-Q30 UT ultrapure water system (Zhiang Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). LED chips (450 nm) were purchased from Looking Long Technology Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China).

The hydrothermal synthesis was performed as follows. A solution of dehydroabietic acid (0.4 g) and ethanolamine (1.0 mL) in deionized water (70 mL) was heated to 200 °C for 300 min in a Teflon-lined autoclave (volume 100 mL). The 20 groups autoclave combined suspensions (~1500 mL) were freeze dried to provide SSF-CDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using an X'Pert 3 Powder X-ray Diffractometer (Malvern Panalytical Ltd., Malvern, United Kingdom). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a fourier transform infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer co., Ltd., Waltham, USA). Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded in DMSO‑d 6 using an AVACE III HD 500 MHz spectrometer (Bruker Corporation, Karlsruhe, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co. Ltd., New York, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1950 ultraviolet–visible spectrofluorometer (Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Photoluminescence (PL) was measured with an LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Co., Ltd., Waltham, USA). Photoluminescence quantum yield and fluorescence decay curves was measured using an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., Edinburgh, UK). LED parameters were measured using an OHSP350 spectrometer (Hongpu Photoelectric Technology Co. Ltd., Hangzhou, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of WLED based on SSF-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The WLED was fabricated using a blue chip (450 nm), as the excitation source and SSF-CDs as the color conversion layer. The SSF-CDs were dispersed in epoxy resin, with a weight/weight ratio of SSF-CDs to epoxy resin of 1:3, and the mixture was coated onto the blue chip. The WLED was obtained after drying at 80 °C for 120 min. The emission spectrum of the WLED was measured at a forward current of 20 mA.

The morphology and crystalline structure of the SSF-CDs were studied by TEM and XRD. A large proportion of the nanoparticles were spherical, with a mean particle diameter of 3.2 nm (Fig. 1 a). The particle size distribution is shown in Fig. 1b. In the high resolution TEM image of the SSF-CDs (Fig. 1a inset), lattice spacings of 0.21 and 0.18 could be clearly identified and were attributed to the (100) and (102) crystal faces of graphite carbon, respectively [49,50]. A conclusion could be obtained by XRD (Fig. S1) that the precursors have undergone a certain degree of carbonization, in which a reduction in peak intensity of the SSF-CDs can be attributed to the transition from dehydroabietic acid to SSF-CDs. No clear diffraction peak appeared in the XRD spectrum for the graphite phase, and the peaks in the spectrum of the SSF-CDs were very similar to those in the spectrum of dehydroabietic acid, which should be attributed to the partially retained structure of the dehydroabietic acid. Bearing this in mind, the SSF-CDs were proposed to contain graphitizing core and tricyclic phenanthrene skeleton.

To confirm this hypothesis, the structure of the SSF-CDs was investigated using FT-IR and 1H NMR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of the SSF-CDs (Fig. 1c) showed a clearly distinguishable peak at 1030 cm−1, which was attributed to the bending of aromatic C–H bonds [31], and absorption peaks at 1468 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibrations of aromatic rings. The aromatic skeleton of dehydroabietic acid is retained in the SSF-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of dehydroabietic acid exhibited a sharp peak at 1687 cm−1, which was attributed to the CO bond of the carboxylic acid group [51,52]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the SSF-CDs, the peak at 1687 cm−1 was replaced by a peak of 1622 cm−1, confirming that the ethanolamine has reacted with the carboxylic acid group of dehydroabietic acid to form an amide bond during the hydrothermal process [26]. A new peak at 1389 cm−1 was attributed to vibrations of C–N bonds and a broad absorption band at 3200–3500 cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibrations of –NH–/–OH bonds [53]. By comparison of the 1H NMR spectra of the SSF-CDs and dehydroabietic acid, the structure that a large number of the functional groups are also part of the SSF-CDs could be implied. Both spectra showed essentially similar signals for the tricyclic phenanthrene skeleton (Fig. 1d and e), together with signals at 6.7–7.2 ppm, which correspond to aromatic H atoms [29]. The signal at 12.0 ppm, corresponding to the carboxylic acid group (–COOH) of dehydroabietic acid, was not present in the 1H NMR spectrum of the SSF-CDs. The spectrum of the SSF-CDs showed signals at 4.2–5.7 ppm and 3.4 ppm, which correspond to aliphatic H hydrogens from ethanolamine, together with a signal at 2.5 ppm, which corresponds to the hydroxy group (–OH). The FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra thus confirm that the biomass-based SSF-CDs retain the tricyclic phenanthrene skeleton of dehydroabietic acid and also contain a variety of functional groups.

XPS was used to further investigate the chemical composition of the SFF-CDs. The full XPS survey spectrum indicated the presence of C, N and O (Fig. 1f and Table S1). The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) showed a single peak at 531.2 eV, which was attributed to CO [25]. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) showed peaks at 284.5, 285.7 and 288.7 eV, which were assigned to CC/C–C, C–O/C–N and CO, respectively [54–56]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1i) displayed peaks at 398.0, 399.3 and 400.2 eV, which were assigned to pyridine N, amino N and pyrrole N, respectively [22,23,57]. The XPS spectra thus indicated that a graphitic carbon core was formed during the hydrothermal process and that the SSF-CDs contain functional groups such as –OH, –NH– and –CONH–, consistent with the FT-IR and 1H NMR spectra.

The SSF-CDs are readily soluble in ethanol, and the solution is clear even when concentration up to 30 mg mL−1 (Fig. S5). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of a solution of SSF-CDs in ethanol (Fig. S6) showed peaks at 267 and 275 nm, which were assigned to the π-π* transition of the aromatic rings [26,62]. An absorption tail at the range of 300–350 nm is consistent with the n-π* transition of CO bonds formed during the hydrothermal process [63]. The PL emission spectra of a dilute solution of SSF-CDs in ethanol (1 mg mL−1) possessed a maximum emission intensity at 433 nm when the excitation wavelengths in the range 300–440 nm (Fig. 3 a). The PL excitation (PLE) spectrum recorded by monitoring the PL peak at 433 nm showed a maximum at 365 nm, in good agreement with the PL spectrum (Fig. S7). The solution showed excitation-dependent behavior, and a marked red-shift observed in the normalized fluorescence spectra (Fig. S8) supported this behavior. The fluorescence emission of the solution of SSF-CDs in ethanol (1 mg mL−1) also fitted a double-exponential decay model, with an average lifetime of 4.85 ns (Fig. 3b), demonstrating that the emission was contributed by surface fluorophores and graphitizing cores [49]. The fluorescence intensity of a solution of SSF-CDs in ethanol decreased slightly as the temperature increased from 30 to 70 °C (Fig. S9), implying that the SSF-CDs are relatively stable at higher temperatures. The solution of SSF-CDs was further investigated as a fluorescent ink. Letters and figures were printed onto filter paper by placing an ethanol solution of SSF-CDs in the ink box of the printer. No images were visible in the normally lit field (Fig. S10, left panels) but the letters and figures fluoresced under a UV lamp (Fig. S10, right panels), demonstrating the potential of SSF-CDs for use in fluorescent inks.

Solutions of SSF-CDs in ethanol showed excitation-dependent behavior regardless of concentration (Fig. S11). As the concentration increased, the emission spectra also exhibited an overall trend towards maximum emission at longer wavelength (Fig. 3c). Solutions of SSF-CDs showed tunable fluorescence emission behavior. Photographs and red-shift (Figs. S12 and S13) in normalized spectra illustrated this behavior. At relatively low concentrations (1–5 mg mL−1), the fluorescent emission peak of the SSF-CDs was centered at ~440 nm, while at higher concentrations (10–20 mg mL−1), the peak was red shifted to ~490 nm, and even at higher concentrations (25–30 mg mL−1), the emission peak was still centered at ~504 nm, at a concentration of 25 mg mL−1, the emission spectrum of the solution was very similar to that of powdered SSF-CDs (Fig. 2a). The above phenomenon showed that the emission underwent a red shift, which was contributed to the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [25,49]. The average fluorescence lifetime of powdered SSF-CDs is shorter than that of SSF-CDs solutions (Table S3), also indicating the occurrence of FRET [49]. In addition, CIE chromaticity coordinates of solutions of SSF-CDs with different concentrations, calculated from their PL spectra (Fig. S11), are shown in Fig. 3d and Table S2 and also illustrated a trend for the color of the solution to change from blue to light-yellow.

The effect of increasing concentration on the fluorescence intensity of ethanol solutions of SSF-CDs was shown in Fig. 3c and e. The fluorescence intensity enhanced with the concentration increased up to 20 mg mL−1 and then decreased slightly, rather than showing quenching, as the concentration was further increased to 25 mg mL−1 and 30 mg mL−1. This phenomenon should be attributed to the self-absorption, which is enhanced at higher concentrations and causes a decrease in PL intensity [64]. Although the direct π-π interactions in the carbon cores would typically cause ACQ, SSF-CDs not only possess excitation-dependent and tunable fluorescence emission, but also produce bright light-yellow fluorescence, even when aggregated in more concentrated solutions. Based on the characterizations of the SSF-CDs, it is reasonable to believe that they consist of graphitic cores, sterically hindered tricyclic phenanthrene skeletons and various functional groups. There are two main explanations for the solid-state fluorescence of the SSF-CDs. Firstly, π-π interactions between carbon cores in the SSF-CDs is reduced by the sterically hindered tricyclic phenanthrene skeleton, which keeps the π-π interactions regions apart [61]. Secondly, oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surfaces of the SSF-CDs may help to avoid ACQ [59]. Due to reduced π-π interactions, ACQ is thus suppressed, allowing the SSF-CDs to exhibit solid-state fluorescence. The mechanisms for solid-state emission and energy transfer of the SSF-CDs are shown in Fig. 3f and g. Since WLEDs can be fabricated using blue light-emitting diode chips as the excitation source to excite yellow or yellow-green solid-state fluorescent carbon dots [49,59,64], the SSF-CDs described here could potentiality be used to fabricate WLEDs.

Using the well-known principle that white light can be produced by mixing blue light and yellow light, a WLED using a blue (450 nm) LED chip was fabricated as the excitation source, yellow-light SSF-CDs as the color conversion layer and epoxy encapsulation (Fig. 4 a). This method is simple and convenient. The blue light LED could be tuned to white light, with color coordinates (0.3304, 0.3055), when the weight/weight ratio of SSF-CDs to epoxy resin was 1:3. A photograph of the WLED operating at a forward current of 20 mA is shown in Fig. 4b (inset). The emission spectrum of a WLED was showed in Fig. 4c, had a correlated color temperature of 5608 K and a color rendering index of 88.6. The luminous efficacy of WLED is 1.36 lm/W at 20 mA. In addition, two photographs of pen caps and LEDs taken under SSF-CDs-based WLED were shown in Fig. 4d and e. Clearly, true colors of the pen caps and LEDs can be obtained. Therefore, the prepared SSF-CDs can thus be used as a color conversion layer in WLEDs.

In summary, a high yield (~100%) strategy from sustainable materials was developed to prepare biomass-based SSF-CDs with a PLQY of 10%. The SSF-CDs were readily soluble in ethanol, and the solutions showed excitation-dependent and tunable fluorescence emission. The SSF-CDs contained graphitic cores with sterically hindered non-planar tricyclic phenanthrene skeletons and a variety of functional groups. The tricyclic phenanthrene skeleton, which reduced π-π interactions between carbon cores in the SSF-CDs by keeping regions of π-π interactions apart, suppressed ACQ and promotes solid-state fluorescence. The high yield made this strategy very suitable for large-scale production of biomass-based SSF-CDs at low cost. More importantly, the biomass-based SSF-CDs was proved to successfully adopted to fabricate a WLED, with CIE chromaticity coordinates (0.3304, 0.3055) when operated at 20 mA.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31700502), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572020BB07) and the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2018QNRC001). The authors are grateful for the funding.
